metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5) specifying and testing that verify_authorship returns a non-verified Verdict (rather than raising) when resolve_trust_root / show_ticket itself raises or returns unexpectedly, and clarify whether resolve_trust_root should catch show_ticket exceptions and return None.
findings.finding: The 'never raises' contract on verify_authorship is only tested for the 'not an identity ticket' and 'empty keys' cases; if rebar.show_ticket itself raises (e.g., unknown id throws rather than returns None, corrupted repo, I/O error), the behavior of resolve_trust_root and verify_authorship is neither specified nor covered by any AC, leaving a live failure path untested.
findings.location: AC5 / What §1 resolve_trust_root / verify_authorship
findings.suggested_fix: In resolve_trust_root, wrap the show_ticket call in a try/except and return None on any exception (with the error reason surfaced in the Verdict returned by verify_authorship). Add an AC: 'If show_ticket raises for any reason, verify_authorship returns a non-verified Verdict and does not propagate the exception.'

findings.finding: AC1 asserts that 'a test asserts `registry.resolve("rebar.authorship.v1")` returns that policy' but does not specify that the test must verify auto-registration at import without an explicit call to `register_authorship_policy()`. A test that calls `register_authorship_policy()` explicitly would satisfy AC1 even if the `__init__.py` wiring described in the plan is entirely absent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / AC1; What / §1 registration wiring
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC1 to: 'A test that (a) resets/isolates the policy registry, (b) does `import rebar.attest` without calling `register_authorship_policy()` directly, then (c) asserts `registry.resolve("rebar.authorship.v1") == Policy(scheme="sshsig", namespace="rebar.authorship.v1")` — passing proves the __init__.py wiring is live.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the test file (e.g., `tests/rebar/attest/test_authorship.py`) to `file_impact` and include a concrete proving command such as `pytest tests/rebar/attest/test_authorship.py -v` so each AC has an executable evidence command.
findings.finding: No test file path is listed in `file_impact` and no concrete `pytest` invocation (or equivalent) is given for any AC, so there is no proving command that produces evidence on success — only descriptions of what tests should assert.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs) and file_impact section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to file_impact: `tests/rebar/attest/test_authorship.py` (new: test_policy_registration, test_round_trip, test_cross_identity_rejection, test_tamper_rejection, test_unknown_identity). Add proving command: `pytest tests/rebar/attest/test_authorship.py -v` to the AC block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit exception handling in `resolve_trust_root` (catch all exceptions from `rebar.show_ticket`) and return `None` (or a non-verified Verdict) on I/O/parse failure, so `verify_authorship` truly never raises for any ticket-store failure mode.
findings.finding: `resolve_trust_root` calls `rebar.show_ticket` but the plan specifies no exception handling for I/O/parse errors from that call, yet AC5 and `verify_authorship`'s docstring promise 'never raises' — an unhandled exception from the ticket store would break that contract.
findings.location: Section 1 — `resolve_trust_root` / `verify_authorship`; AC: 'Unknown/keyless identity: verify_authorship … never raises'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `show_ticket` call in a try/except Exception block inside `resolve_trust_root`; on any exception, log a warning (e.g. `logger.warning("Failed to load identity %s: %s", identity_id, e)`) and return `None`, letting the caller receive a non-verified Verdict rather than a crash.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `resolve_trust_root` must catch `RebarError` (and any other exception from `rebar.show_ticket`) and return `None` in all failure modes, so `verify_authorship` is guaranteed never to raise for an unresolvable identity id (AC-5).
findings.finding: resolve_trust_root does not specify catching RebarError for absent ticket ids, yet rebar.show_ticket raises RebarError when a ticket id cannot be resolved — this breaks the 'never raises' contract required by AC-5 for unknown identities.
findings.location: What §1 — resolve_trust_root; Acceptance Criteria AC-5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the resolve_trust_root spec: 'Wrap the rebar.show_ticket call in a try/except (RebarError, Exception) and return None on any error, so callers can always treat None as the definitive "nothing to trust" signal regardless of whether the id exists in the store.'

findings.finding: resolve_trust_root does not specify how exceptions raised by rebar.show_ticket (e.g., IO error, malformed ticket data, missing repo) are handled, potentially violating the AC guarantee that verify_authorship 'never raises' for unknown/keyless identities.
findings.location: What / resolve_trust_root; Acceptance Criteria — 'Unknown/keyless identity' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the rebar.show_ticket call in a try/except that catches at minimum OSError and any parse/validation exceptions, returns None on failure, and emits a structured log line (e.g., `logger.warning('resolve_trust_root: failed to load identity %s: %s', identity_id, e)`). Document in the plan which exception types are swallowed vs. re-raised (e.g., only ValueError/OSError are swallowed; unexpected errors still propagate).

findings.finding: The plan's `resolve_trust_root` (and thereby `verify_authorship`) is specified to return `None` / a non-verified Verdict for an unknown identity, but the plan does not specify exception-handling for `rebar.show_ticket` itself raising (e.g., a `RebarError` / `KeyError` if the ticket id does not exist). The AC says 'never raises', but the plan's prose only covers the conditional `None`-return path, not the exception-propagation path from the underlying store call.
findings.location: Section 'What → resolve_trust_root'; AC line 'Unknown/keyless identity'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Wrap the show_ticket call in try/except Exception: return None so that a missing/invalid ticket id is treated the same as an unknown identity.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the `attest` → `rebar.show_ticket` cross-component dependency in the plan (or Scope section), justify why it belongs in the substrate layer rather than in a higher-level adapter, and specify a mitigation (e.g. accept a `keys: list[str]` parameter to decouple resolution from ticket-loading, or inject a `key_resolver` callable).
findings.finding: `authorship.py` is placed inside the `attest` foundation substrate but calls `rebar.show_ticket` — a higher-level application function — creating an upward/inverted dependency from substrate to application layer that is not acknowledged, justified, or mitigated by an interface boundary.
findings.location: Section 1 — `resolve_trust_root`; Section 'Scope'
findings.suggested_fix: Consider making `resolve_trust_root` accept a `keys: list[str]` directly (pure function, no I/O), and move the `show_ticket` lookup to a thin adapter in the higher-level `rebar` layer — keeping `attest` substrate free of application-layer dependencies and trivially unit-testable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `allowed_signers_from_keys` must validate (and reject/raise on) a `principal` that contains whitespace or other characters that would corrupt the SSHSIG allowed_signers line format, not just validate the key entries.
findings.finding: allowed_signers_from_keys does not specify sanitizing or rejecting a principal that contains spaces or other SSHSIG allowed_signers format-breaking characters, leaving a latent format-corruption path if a non-standard identity_id ever flows through.
findings.location: What §1 — allowed_signers_from_keys

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete field/attribute and value used to identify an `identity` ticket in `resolve_trust_root` (e.g., `ticket['kind'] == 'identity'` and `ticket['keys']`), so the executor does not need to consult the ddbe spec.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `resolve_trust_root` determines whether a value returned by `rebar.show_ticket` is an 'identity' ticket — no field name, attribute, or duck-type check is given (e.g., `ticket['kind'] == 'identity'` vs `ticket.type` vs some other discriminant), leaving the executor to guess the schema.
findings.location: What > resolve_trust_root / 'if it is an identity ticket with a non-empty keys list'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `resolve_trust_root` description: 'Check `ticket["kind"] == "identity"` (the field name from ddbe) and `ticket["keys"]` for the public-key list; if `show_ticket` raises or returns None, treat as unknown and return None.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'malformed' for `allowed_signers_from_keys` (e.g., 'skip lines that are empty, whitespace-only, or do not start with a recognized SSH key type such as ssh-ed25519/ssh-rsa/ecdsa-*') so the executor has an unambiguous filter rule.
findings.finding: The plan specifies what 'malformed' key entries should be skipped in `allowed_signers_from_keys` but does not define what counts as malformed, leaving the validation boundary undefined (e.g., does a line with no key type prefix count? a line that is only whitespace? a comment line starting with '#'?).
findings.location: What > allowed_signers_from_keys / 'Skip blank/malformed key entries'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A key entry is malformed if it is empty, whitespace-only, or its first token is not a recognized OpenSSH public-key type (ssh-ed25519, ssh-rsa, ecdsa-sha2-nistp256, etc.); skip such entries silently.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'malformed' for allowed_signers_from_keys — specify the minimum structural criterion an authorized-keys line must meet to be included (e.g., non-empty after strip, starts with a recognized key-type token, or passes a split into at least two whitespace-separated fields).
findings.finding: allowed_signers_from_keys specifies 'Skip blank/malformed key entries' but never defines what constitutes a 'malformed' authorized-keys line — no validation criterion, regex, or structural check is given. An executor must invent their own policy, which could produce divergent allowed_signers content and thus divergent verification outcomes.
findings.location: Section 1 / What — allowed_signers_from_keys spec
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the spec: 'A key entry is malformed if stripping it yields an empty string, or if it does not contain at least two whitespace-separated tokens (key-type and base64 blob). Blank lines and comment lines (starting with #) are skipped. All other entries are included verbatim.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact field and value `resolve_trust_root` uses to detect an identity ticket (e.g., `state.get("ticket_type") == "identity"`) and confirm `"identity"` has been added to the `ticket_type` enum in `common.schema.json` before this plan is implemented.
findings.finding: resolve_trust_root is specified to return None 'if it is not an identity ticket,' but the plan never names the field or value used to perform this discrimination. The executor must independently decide whether to check ticket_type == 'identity', presence of a 'keys' field, or something else — and the ticket_type enum in common.schema.json currently does not include 'identity' (it lists bug, epic, story, task, session_log, code_review), so the value vocabulary for this branch is undefined in the current source of truth.
findings.location: Section 1 / What — resolve_trust_root spec; src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json line 13
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the resolve_trust_root spec: 'Load the ticket via rebar.show_ticket; check state.get("ticket_type") == "identity"; if not, return None.' Also reference the ddbe prerequisite that adds "identity" to the common.schema.json enum as a hard dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC1's verification step with the plan body: either name `registry.resolve()` in What §1 (and confirm it exists on the foundation substrate) or rewrite AC1 to verify via `registry.POLICY["rebar.authorship.v1"]` directly.
findings.finding: AC1 verifies the policy registration by calling `registry.resolve("rebar.authorship.v1")`, but the What section describes only `registry.POLICY[AUTHORSHIP_KIND]` (direct dict access) and `registry.verify()` — `registry.resolve()` appears nowhere else in the plan. If `registry.resolve` is not an existing function on the foundation substrate, the AC's verification step references an entity that has no described counterpart, leaving the implementer unable to write the asserted test without guessing at the API.
findings.location: AC1 ('a test asserts `registry.resolve("rebar.authorship.v1")` returns that policy') vs What §1 ('registry.POLICY[AUTHORSHIP_KIND]')
findings.suggested_fix: If `registry.resolve(kind)` is an existing helper on the foundation `registry` module, add a one-line note in What §1 confirming its existence (e.g., 'the foundation `registry` exposes `registry.resolve(kind) -> Policy` as an alias for `registry.POLICY[kind]`'). If it does not exist, update AC1 to read: 'a test asserts `registry.POLICY["rebar.authorship.v1"] == registry.Policy(scheme="sshsig", namespace="rebar.authorship.v1")`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in `resolve_trust_root` that the import must be `from rebar._lib_reads import show_ticket` (or `from rebar import _reads` then `_reads.show_ticket`) — the version that accepts `repo_root` — and add this import to `file_impact` or the What section so the implementer does not accidentally use the public MCP facade which lacks that parameter.
findings.finding: The plan calls `rebar.show_ticket(identity_id, repo_root=...)` inside `resolve_trust_root`, but the public `rebar.show_ticket` facade (re-exported from `rebar.__init__`) does not accept a `repo_root` keyword argument — that parameter only exists on `rebar._reads.show_ticket` and `rebar._lib_reads.show_ticket`. Using the public facade would raise a `TypeError` at runtime whenever `repo_root` is non-None.
findings.location: What / resolve_trust_root — 'load the identity via rebar.show_ticket(identity_id, repo_root=...)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the prose to: 'load the identity via `rebar._lib_reads.show_ticket(identity_id, repo_root=repo_root)` (the internal lib-reads version, which accepts `repo_root`; do not use the MCP-facing public facade)'.

findings.finding: sign_authorship's documented exception contract (`raises SshKeygenUnavailable`) cannot be satisfied by the described mechanism: `sshsig.sign()` calls `subprocess.run(..., check=True)`, which raises `FileNotFoundError` (binary absent) or `CalledProcessError` (bad exit), not `SshKeygenUnavailable`. That exception is only raised by `sshsig.ensure_available()`, which the plan does not mention calling inside `sign_authorship`.
findings.location: What / section 1 / sign_authorship bullet
findings.suggested_fix: At the start of sign_authorship, before calling sshsig.sign(), add: `sshsig.ensure_available()  # raises SshKeygenUnavailable if absent or < 8.9`

findings.finding: The plan asserts sign_authorship 'Raises SshKeygenUnavailable if ssh-keygen is missing/too old', but the underlying sshsig.sign() does NOT call ensure_available() — it uses subprocess.run(..., check=True) which raises subprocess.CalledProcessError (not SshKeygenUnavailable) when ssh-keygen is absent. sign_authorship must explicitly call sshsig.ensure_available() before invoking sshsig.sign() to honour the stated contract.
findings.location: What / §1 sign_authorship — 'Raises sshsig.SshKeygenUnavailable if ssh-keygen is missing/too old (do not swallow — signing must be honest).'
findings.suggested_fix: At the start of sign_authorship: `sshsig.ensure_available()  # raises SshKeygenUnavailable if absent/too old` — then proceed with pae/sign/Envelope construction.

findings.finding: The plan claims `sign_authorship` raises `sshsig.SshKeygenUnavailable` when ssh-keygen is missing or too old, but it never calls `sshsig.ensure_available()` before `sshsig.sign(...)`. The existing `sshsig.sign` uses `subprocess.run(..., check=True)` which raises `FileNotFoundError` (not `SshKeygenUnavailable`) when ssh-keygen is absent. Without an explicit `ensure_available()` call specified in the plan, the stated exception contract is unreachable.
findings.location: What / Section 1 — sign_authorship body: 'Raises sshsig.SshKeygenUnavailable if ssh-keygen is missing/too old (do not swallow — signing must be honest).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `sshsig.ensure_available()` as the first statement in `sign_authorship`'s body, before the `pae = dsse.pae(...)` line. This converts FileNotFoundError / version-check failure into the documented SshKeygenUnavailable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one observability touchpoint to the plan: log a structured warning on `verified=False` outcomes in `verify_authorship` and on `SshKeygenUnavailable` in `sign_authorship` (e.g. `logger.warning("authorship verification failed", extra={"identity_id": ..., "reason": verdict.reason})`)
findings.finding: No observability (logging, metric, or trace) is planned for signing failures or verification failures on this security-critical code path.
findings.location: Section 1 — `sign_authorship`, `verify_authorship`; overall plan

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that ddbe's `identity` ticket-type registration (in `common.schema.json`, `types.py`, and the create path) is fully merged before bde5 implementation begins, and add an explicit AC or file_impact entry stating that `ticket_type == 'identity'` and a `keys: list[str]` field are guaranteed on the `show_ticket` return dict (currently neither appears in the schema or `TicketState` TypedDict).
findings.finding: The plan's foundational premise — 'Builds on ddbe (identity entity holds `keys`)' — relies on `identity` being a registered ticket type and on `show_ticket` returning a `keys` field, but the current `common.schema.json` ticket_type enum does not include `identity` (only bug/epic/story/task/session_log/code_review), and `types.py` `TicketType` likewise omits it. `resolve_trust_root` branches on `ticket['ticket_type'] == 'identity'` and reads `ticket['keys']`; if ddbe's changes are not actually present in the repo files at implementation time, `resolve_trust_root` would always return `None`, silently making `verify_authorship` always return non-verified without error.
findings.location: Section 'Context' ('Builds on ddbe'); Section 1 `resolve_trust_root` ('if it is an `identity` ticket with a non-empty `keys` list')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prerequisite note confirming that `identity` is present in `common.schema.json` enum and `types.py` TicketType before implementing `resolve_trust_root`'s ticket-type guard; verify ddbe's file_impact edits to these files have actually landed.
findings.finding: The plan's `resolve_trust_root` checks whether the ticket returned by show_ticket 'is an `identity` ticket' (i.e., `ticket_type == 'identity'`), but `identity` is not present in the `ticket_type` enum in `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` or `src/rebar/types.py` in the current codebase — even though ddbe (the dependency that was supposed to add it) is marked closed. The plan does not flag this as a prerequisite verification step or note that the implementer must confirm ddbe's `identity` type actually landed.
findings.location: What / resolve_trust_root — 'if it is an `identity` ticket with a non-empty `keys` list'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or document) that ddbe's reducer implementation explicitly folds the `keys` field from identity CREATE events into compiled ticket state, so `rebar.show_ticket` reliably surfaces it; add a cross-reference to the specific file:line in ddbe that performs this fold.
findings.finding: The plan's resolve_trust_root asserts that `rebar.show_ticket(identity_id)` will return a `keys` field for identity tickets, but this is an unverified capability assertion: in the current codebase, the reducer's process_create only explicitly folds a fixed set of named fields into state (ticket_type, title, status, author, parent_id, priority, assignee, alias, description, tags, and provenance source_* keys). The `keys` field (and other identity-specific fields: name, email, mappings) are NOT in this explicit fold list. Surfacing `keys` via show_ticket requires that ddbe's implementation added explicit reducer folding of identity-specific fields — which cannot be confirmed from the codebase as it stands.
findings.location: Section 'What' / resolve_trust_root; src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:process_create
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note citing the specific reducer location (or ddbe commit) where identity-specific fields (keys, name, email, mappings) are folded into state. If this fold is absent, add it to the file_impact or scope of this leaf, or add an integration test in this ticket that calls `rebar.show_ticket` on a real identity ticket and asserts the `keys` field is present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm 'identity' has been added to the `ticket_type` enum in `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` (and regenerated `src/rebar/types.py`) before implementing this plan; if not, list ddbe as an explicit unmerged prerequisite.
findings.finding: The plan's resolve_trust_root branches on the rebar.show_ticket result being an 'identity' ticket, but the source-of-truth schema (common.schema.json line 13) currently enumerates ticket_type as ['bug','epic','story','task','session_log','code_review'] — 'identity' is absent. If ddbe's schema edit has not landed in this codebase snapshot, show_ticket will never return a state with ticket_type='identity', and resolve_trust_root will always fall through to the 'unknown/keyless' path regardless of what identity id is supplied, silently breaking the round-trip AC.
findings.location: Section 1 / resolve_trust_root; src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json line 13
findings.suggested_fix: Add a precondition note in the plan: 'Requires ddbe's schema changes (identity ticket_type enum) to be merged; verify by checking common.schema.json includes "identity" before starting implementation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that ddbe's reducer implementation explicitly folds the 'keys' field into compiled ticket state (i.e., `state['keys'] = data.get('keys', [])` or equivalent in process_create), and add it as a named prerequisite if it is not yet merged.
findings.finding: The plan's resolve_trust_root depends on rebar.show_ticket returning a 'keys' field on identity tickets, but the reducer's process_create (reducer/_processors.py lines 50–96) explicitly folds only named fields into compiled state (ticket_type, title, status, author, created_at, env_id, parent_id, priority, assignee, alias, description, tags, source_* provenance) — there is no explicit handling for 'keys'. If ddbe's reducer work did not add explicit folding of 'keys' into state, show_ticket().get('keys') will return None for all identity tickets, making allowed_signers_from_keys unreachable.
findings.location: Section 1 / resolve_trust_root and allowed_signers_from_keys; src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py lines 50-96

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
